content.text: Workload Identity Federation (WIF) lets an external identity — a GitHub Actions runner, a Kubernetes pod, a GCP service account, or any OIDC-compliant workload — authenticate to Microsoft Entra ID without storing any credential. Instead of presenting a client secret or certificate, the workload presents a short-lived OIDC token issued by its own identity provider. Entra ID exchanges that token for an Entra ID access token based on a trust relationship you configure in the App Registration.

attrs.alt: WIF flow: External IdP issues OIDC token → workload exchanges it with Entra ID → receives Azure access token — no stored credentials

content.text: The trust between an external identity provider and Entra ID is established through a Federated Identity Credential — a configuration object on an App Registration (or User-Assigned Managed Identity) that specifies which external issuer and subject are trusted. At authentication time, Entra ID validates the external token's signature by fetching the issuer's JWKS endpoint, checks that the subject claim matches the configured value, and issues an Entra ID access token.
Configure a Federated Identity Credential on the App Registration: issuer URL, subject, audience.
The workload receives an OIDC token from its own identity provider (GitHub, AKS, GCP, etc.).
The workload POSTs the external OIDC token to the Entra ID token endpoint with grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer.
Entra ID fetches the issuer's JWKS endpoint to verify the token signature.
Entra ID checks that the subject claim matches the configured federated credential.
If valid, Entra ID issues a JWT access token for the requested Azure scope.
The workload uses the Entra ID token as a Bearer header on Azure API calls.

The external OIDC token is typically valid for 5–10 minutes. It is never stored — it is created fresh on each job or workflow run by the identity provider, used once to exchange for an Entra ID token, and discarded.

GitHub Actions is the most common WIF use case. With OIDC enabled, the runner receives a GitHub-signed JWT that identifies the repository, branch, and workflow. This replaces AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET or certificate files entirely.
Step 1 — Configure the Federated Identity Credential

APP_OBJ_ID='<app-registration-object-id>'

# Trust GitHub Actions on the main branch
az ad app federated-credential create \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --parameters '{
        "name": "github-main",
        "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
        "subject": "repo:my-org/my-repo:ref:refs/heads/main",
        "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"],
        "description": "GitHub Actions main branch deployments"
    }'

# Trust pull requests (different subject format)
az ad app federated-credential create \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --parameters '{
        "name": "github-prs",
        "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
        "subject": "repo:my-org/my-repo:pull_request",
        "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
    }'

# Trust a specific environment (safer than branch/PR alone)
az ad app federated-credential create \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --parameters '{
        "name": "github-production-env",
        "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
        "subject": "repo:my-org/my-repo:environment:production",
        "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
    }'

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy to Azure

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  id-token: write   # required — allows the runner to request OIDC token
  contents: read

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production  # restricts to production federated credential

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id:       ${{ vars.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}      # not a secret
          tenant-id:       ${{ vars.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}      # not a secret
          subscription-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }} # not a secret
          # No client-secret or certificate

      - name: Deploy Bicep template
        run: |
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group MyRG \
            --template-file infra/main.bicep

      - name: Use SDK (DefaultAzureCredential works in Actions with OIDC)
        run: python3 scripts/post-deploy.py

In Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), Workload Identity Federation replaces the older AAD Pod Identity add-on. Pods are linked to a Kubernetes service account, which is federated with a User-Assigned Managed Identity (or App Registration). The webhook injects the OIDC token and client ID automatically.

# 1. Enable OIDC issuer and workload identity on the cluster
az aks update \
    --resource-group MyRG --name MyCluster \
    --enable-oidc-issuer \
    --enable-workload-identity

OIDC_ISSUER=$(az aks show --resource-group MyRG --name MyCluster \
    --query oidcIssuerProfile.issuerUrl -o tsv)

# 2. Create user-assigned managed identity
az identity create --resource-group MyRG --name my-workload-identity
CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group MyRG --name my-workload-identity --query clientId -o tsv)
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az identity show --resource-group MyRG --name my-workload-identity --query principalId -o tsv)

# 3. Create federated credential linking K8s service account to the identity
az identity federated-credential create \
    --name aks-workload-fed \
    --identity-name my-workload-identity \
    --resource-group MyRG \
    --issuer "$OIDC_ISSUER" \
    --subject 'system:serviceaccount:my-namespace:my-service-account' \
    --audience 'api://AzureADTokenExchange'

# 4. Grant the identity access to Azure resources
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show --name MyVault --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
    --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
    --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
    --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
    --scope "$KV_ID"

# service-account.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: my-service-account
  namespace: my-namespace
  annotations:
    azure.workload.identity/client-id: "<user-assigned-identity-client-id>"
---
# pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: my-namespace
  labels:
    azure.workload.identity/use: 'true'   # required — triggers webhook injection
spec:
  serviceAccountName: my-service-account
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest
      # Webhook injects: AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_TENANT_ID,
      # AZURE_FEDERATED_TOKEN_FILE, AZURE_AUTHORITY_HOST
      # DefaultAzureCredential reads these automatically

# In the AKS pod: DefaultAzureCredential detects workload identity env vars
# and uses WorkloadIdentityCredential automatically
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = SecretClient('https://my-vault.vault.azure.net', credential)
secret = client.get_secret('my-secret')
print(secret.value)

# No client_id, no secret, no certificate — the webhook-injected env vars
# and the projected service account token handle everything

WIF supports any OIDC-compliant identity provider. The configuration pattern is the same — configure the federated credential with the provider's issuer URL and the subject format specific to that provider.
Using WIF with User-Assigned Managed Identity

Federated Identity Credentials can be configured on a User-Assigned Managed Identity as well as on an App Registration. This is the recommended approach for AKS workload identity because the managed identity can be granted RBAC roles directly, without needing to register an explicit application.

# Create a federated credential on a user-assigned managed identity
# (alternative to using an App Registration)
IDENTITY_RESOURCE_ID=$(az identity show \
    --resource-group MyRG --name my-workload-identity --query id -o tsv)

az rest --method POST \
    --uri "${IDENTITY_RESOURCE_ID}/federatedIdentityCredentials?api-version=2023-01-31" \
    --body '{
        "name": "github-main",
        "properties": {
            "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
            "subject": "repo:my-org/my-repo:ref:refs/heads/main",
            "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
        }
    }'

# List federated credentials on the identity
az rest --method GET \
    --uri "${IDENTITY_RESOURCE_ID}/federatedIdentityCredentials?api-version=2023-01-31" \
    --query 'value[].{Name:name, Issuer:properties.issuer, Subject:properties.subject}'

// Track Entra ID token issuance via federated identity credential exchange
// These appear as sign-ins with idTokenHint or federation claim
AADServicePrincipalSignInLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where ResourceDisplayName has 'AzureADTokenExchange'
    or IncomingTokenType == 'selfSignedJwt'
| summarize
    Exchanges = count(),
    Workloads = make_set(ServicePrincipalName),
    LastSeen  = max(TimeGenerated)
    by ServicePrincipalId, ResourceDisplayName
| order by Exchanges desc

// Federated credential configuration changes (additions / removals)
AuditLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where OperationName has 'federatedIdentityCredential'
    or OperationName has 'Add federated identity credential'
| extend AppName = tostring(TargetResources[0].displayName)
| extend Actor   = tostring(InitiatedBy.user.userPrincipalName)
| project TimeGenerated, OperationName, AppName, Actor, Result
| order by TimeGenerated desc
Comparison: WIF vs Client Secret vs Certificate
No — OIDC issuer JWKS must be reachable by Entra ID
Medium — IdP config + fed cred config
Terraform — Configuring WIF with azuread Provider

Terraform's azuread provider has native support for federated identity credentials. This lets you configure the trust relationship as part of the same IaC run that creates the app registration and role assignments.
Cross-Cloud: GCP Service Account Accessing Azure

WIF enables cross-cloud trust. A GCP service account running in Google Cloud Run or Compute Engine can access Azure resources without any Azure credentials stored in GCP. The GCP service account's OIDC token (issued by accounts.google.com) is exchanged for an Entra ID access token.

# Configure Entra ID to trust a GCP service account
GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT='my-service@my-project.iam.gserviceaccount.com'
APP_OBJ_ID='<app-registration-object-id>'

az ad app federated-credential create \
    --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --parameters "{
        \"name\": \"gcp-service-account\",
        \"issuer\": \"https://accounts.google.com\",
        \"subject\": \"$GCP_SERVICE_ACCOUNT\",
        \"audiences\": [\"api://AzureADTokenExchange\"]
    }"

# Python code running on GCP — access Azure Key Vault without stored credentials
import google.auth
import google.auth.transport.requests
import requests
from azure.identity import WorkloadIdentityCredential

# GCP obtains its own OIDC token for the Azure audience
gcp_credentials, project = google.auth.default()
auth_req = google.auth.transport.requests.Request()
gcp_credentials.refresh(auth_req)

# Exchange GCP OIDC token for Entra ID token
TENANT_ID = '<tenant-id>'
CLIENT_ID = '<app-client-id>'

resp = requests.post(
    f'https://login.microsoftonline.com/{TENANT_ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token',
    data={
        'grant_type': 'urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer',
        'client_id': CLIENT_ID,
        'client_assertion_type': 'urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer',
        'client_assertion': gcp_credentials.token,
        'requested_token_use': 'on_behalf_of',
        'scope': 'https://vault.azure.net/.default',
    }
)
azure_token = resp.json()['access_token']
print('Got Azure token:', azure_token[:20], '...')
Maximum 20 federated identity credentials per App Registration.
Maximum 20 federated identity credentials per User-Assigned Managed Identity.
The issuer's JWKS endpoint must be publicly reachable by Entra ID. Private OIDC issuers (internal cluster without public endpoint) are not supported.
The audience value api://AzureADTokenExchange is required; custom audiences are not supported.
WIF does not support personal Microsoft accounts (MSA) — only organizational Entra ID accounts.
The exchanged Entra ID access token has the same lifetime as any other access token (~60–75 minutes); it is cached by the Azure SDK automatically.
Subject claim matching is exact string equality — wildcards are not supported.

If you need more than 20 federated credentials for the same application (e.g. many repositories deploying to the same service principal), use a separate service principal per group of related repositories, or use environment-scoped subjects to reduce the count.

# Common error: AADSTS70021 — no matching federated identity found
# Cause: subject claim in the incoming OIDC token does not match any configured credential

# Decode the incoming OIDC token to inspect the subject claim
OIDC_TOKEN='<token-from-external-provider>'
echo "$OIDC_TOKEN" | cut -d'.' -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | python3 -m json.tool

# Check configured federated credentials
APP_OBJ_ID='<app-registration-object-id>'
az ad app federated-credential list --id "$APP_OBJ_ID" \
    --query '[].{Name:name, Issuer:issuer, Subject:subject}'

# Common mismatches for GitHub Actions:
# - Using repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main but workflow runs on a PR
#   Fix: add a separate credential with subject repo:org/repo:pull_request
# - Using environment name that doesn't match the workflow environment: field
# - Typo in org/repo name (case-sensitive)

Workload Identity Federation eliminates stored credentials from CI/CD and container workloads. Instead of a client secret or certificate file, the workload presents a short-lived OIDC token from its own identity provider. Entra ID verifies the token using the issuer's public JWKS endpoint and exchanges it for an Entra ID access token, based on a Federated Identity Credential configured on the App Registration or User-Assigned Managed Identity. GitHub Actions, AKS workload identity, GitLab CI, CircleCI, Terraform Cloud, and cross-cloud workloads (GCP, AWS) all support this pattern. The only setup artifact is the trust mapping — issuer URL, subject claim, and audience. There is nothing to rotate, nothing to leak, and no secrets stored anywhere in the CI/CD environment.
